figures.caption: Figure 1: 1D source-term samples from the three diffusion training distributions. Datasets 2 and 3 both cover the full coarse space; Dataset 3 has the broadest spectral content.

Figure 2: Mean F-GMRES iterations for diffusion problems, averaged over 1000 realisations. Bars: discretisation-dependent tolerance with the in-distribution right-hand side; lines: tolerance 10−10 with random right-hand side. Missing entries indicate consistent convergence failure; the dashed line is the iteration limit (5000).

Figure 5: The first four vectors passed to the exact A-DEF1 preconditioner during F-GMRES on diffusion problems from Datasets 1 and 3, reshaped to the discretisation grid. Even when the original source term is smooth (Dataset 1, top row), later GMRES iterations produce vectors of broader spectral content that the preconditioner must handle.

Figure 6: Eigenvalues of preconditioned systems for the six preconditioners (smoother only, exact, DeepONet, RINO, VarMiON, NGO), applied to a 40× 40 discretisation of diffusion with θ ≡ 1.0. Sampling-based architectures (DeepONet, VarMiON) produce structurally non-symmetric spectra; integration-based ones (RINO, NGO) match the (symmetric) spectrum of the exact preconditioner.

Figure 7: The Green’s function of a 1D diffusion problem (left) and the structural form of the four learned preconditioners. The integration-based architectures (middle) are symmetric in their row/column structure; the sampling-based architectures (right) have a row space concentrated at the sensor nodes, breaking symmetry irrespective of training quality.

Figure 8: Left: mean PCG iterations for 80× 80 diffusion problems, by coarse-correction architecture and combination form. The DeepONet- and VarMiON-based corrections are omitted because PCG consistently failed on them. Right: convergence history for various A-DEF2 preconditioners on a representative problem, showing that the RINO and unsymmetrised NGO preconditioners result in stagnation of PCG.

Figure 9: Mean F-GMRES iterations for advection-diffusion. Bars: discretisation-dependent tolerance, in-distribution right-hand side; lines: tolerance 10−10, random right-hand side. The NGO matches the exact two-level preconditioner on every mesh and on both data sets.

Table 2: Average F-GMRES iterations and number of failures for different preconditioners on 80 × 80 discretisations of the advection-diffusion equation. The average iteration counts now exclude the cases where convergence was not achieved.
Figure 11: Convergence histories of F-GMRES on representative advection-diffusion problems at low and high Péclet number.

Figure 12: Spectra of preconditioned systems for advection-diffusion on a 40 × 40 discretisation with θ ≡ 0.1 and c = (0.6, 0.8) (Pe = 10). The NGO and RINO both produce well-clustered spectra; the NGO’s is tighter.

Figure 13: Helmholtz preconditioning. Left: average F-GMRES iterations for various discretisations and preconditioners. Right: F-GMRES iterations on the 80 × 80 mesh as a function of the average wave number. The DeepONet is omitted because it failed to converge in every test.

Figure 14: Helmholtz preconditioned spectra on an 80× 80 mesh. At k = 5 the problem is close to positive definite and all three viable architectures cluster near [1, 2]; at k = 20 the exact preconditioner remains well-conditioned but all three learned ones acquire near-imaginary eigenvalues.

Figure 15: Convergence histories of F-GMRES for representative Helmholtz problems on the 80×80 mesh. The exact coarse solve maintains monotone convergence at both wave numbers; the learned methods deteriorate at high k.

Figure 16: Left: average time taken to solve 1000 diffusion problems using conjugate gradients with various preconditioners. Right: breakdown of the computation time required to solve 80× 80 discretisations of diffusion.
Figure 17: Example of a diffusion problem from the three training data sets.
Figure 18: Example of an advection-diffusion problem from the training data set (top) and the out-of-distribution testing data set (bottom).
Figure 19: Example of a Helmholtz problem from the training data set. For g and η, the solid and dashed lines indicate the real and imaginary parts, respectively.
Figure 20: The convolutional kernel used as a smoother for the diffusion and Helmholtz equations.
Figure 21: The four sweep directions used by the four-direction Gauss-Seidel smoother.

Figure 22: Architectures of the four neural operators considered in this work. The order matches the design grid of Table 1: DeepONet (sample-and-non-linear), RINO (integrate-and-non-linear), VarMiON (sample-and-linear), NGO (integrate-and-linear).
